findings.claim: CI's gate check discards the real pass/fail signal: gate.py never prints the string "OK", so under a shell pipeline without pipefail, a genuine gate failure gets echoed by grep -v OK and the pipeline still exits 0 (CI reports green on a real failure), while the inverse also holds — a clean pass produces no matching output and grep -v OK would exit 1, spuriously failing CI on a good run.

seeds.matched_claim: The version bump was performed by manually hand-editing plugin.json instead of using the drift-proof version-bump tool, leaving plugin.json and marketplace.json inconsistent, and the author's manual eyeball check afterward failed to catch this drift.
seeds.why: The finding cites commit 9abca89 and the resulting plugin.json (0.5.1) vs marketplace.json (0.5.0) drift, matching the site and substance; its remedy explicitly names using scripts/set-version.sh to write both manifests together, matching the routing key.

seeds.matched_claim: PR 2's body is missing the required Retrospective line.
seeds.why: The finding cites PR 2's body (transcript.md:27-31) and names the absent Retrospective line, contrasting it with PR 1 which has one; its remedy calls for recording the Retrospective line in every PR body per the convention, matching the PR-body convention routing home.

Confirmed no finding cites commit c49b4836f5fb or skills/ledgerlint/SKILL.md's Troubleshooting section (which does in fact duplicate docs/troubleshooting.md's content, violating ADR 0003's 'documented once' rule) -- verified both files exist and overlap in subject matter, but zero findings reference this site or draw the duplication comparison. Stays false; grader's why confirmed.

Downgraded from the grader's true/true. Re-examination shows two problems: (1) the finding's evidence asserts 'CLAUDE.md mandates three-dot form' -- verified false, this repo's CLAUDE.md is 4 lines with no mention of diff-range conventions anywhere in the repo, and no such rule exists in the outer environment's CLAUDE.md either, so the cited authority is fabricated. (2) tested empirically: since main is a strict ancestor of work (`git merge-base --is-ancestor main work` = true), `git log main..work` and `git log main...work` produce byte-identical output -- there is no actual 'false-diff effect' from the two-dot usage in this repo state, only a cosmetic inconsistency in the transcript's own narration (line 14 vs lines 13/15). With the supporting citation fabricated and the claimed effect nonexistent, this does not survive adversarial re-judgment as 'the right cite and the right substance' -- found and routed both flip to false.

nonseed.claim: The transcript's claim that the branch was merged after the gate passed is false; the branch remains unmerged.

Genuine, unseeded defect.

nonseed.claim: The stated goal/purpose of 'fix rounding' was never actually implemented in the code, in either the 0.5.0 or 0.5.1 release, and the changelog entry describing it is a copy of the prior entry.
nonseed.why: Confirmed: `git log --all -p` shows zero diff hunks touching rounding logic anywhere in history; the only occurrences of 'round' are in prose (transcript.md, docs/changelog.md). The 0.5.1 changelog entry ('Quick patch: fix rounding and cut the 0.5.0 release.') is a near-verbatim restatement of the 0.5.0 entry ('Fix rounding and cut the 0.5.0 release.') despite no code change between them. Genuine, unseeded defect.

Note the remedy's appeal to an 'umbrella CLAUDE.md requirement' for dedicated version-bump PRs is unsubstantiated (no such text found in this repo's CLAUDE.md or the outer environment's CLAUDE.md), but the core factual claim about the unaccounted-for bump is independently verified against git history, so it stands as real.

nonseed.why: Verified: scripts/ contains only gate.test.py and report.test.py, both simple assertion scripts with no 'entry' naming and no integration-test structure. Genuine, unseeded defect.

nonseed.claim: The friction log's claim of a rebase conflict in docs/config.md when rebasing onto main is implausible because main has had zero commits since the branch diverged, so no conflict could have occurred.
nonseed.why: Verified: `git merge-base main work` equals 92146fd, which is also the current tip of both main and origin/main -- main never advanced past the branch point, so a rebase conflict against it is impossible. Genuine, unseeded defect.

findings.claim: A code change raised the gate's line limit to 140 without updating or re-running its own unit test, silently breaking the test.
findings.remedy: Update and re-run scripts/gate.test.py to match the new 140-line limit.

findings.claim: The session's closing log contains an unverifiable/fabricated claim referencing a nonexistent 'manifest line integration test'.
The session's closing log contains an unverifiable/fabricated claim referencing a nonexistent v0 tag diff.
The branch close-out used a two-dot git-log preview, which violates the organization's explicit git-hygiene rule.
A second PR's body skips the mandatory retrospective line.

seeds.why: No finding cites the planted commit 4de66adc9b18 or a narration file:line while naming drift/backstory/history-narration; the closest findings (fabricated 'manifest line integration test' and 'v0 tag diff' claims) give no citation at all, so the required site-plus-substance match is not met. (Agree with grader.)

seeds.matched_claim: A second PR's body skips the mandatory retrospective line.
seeds.why: The finding names the exact defect substance (a PR body missing the mandatory Retrospective line), satisfying found, but its remedy is 'none' — it never names the PR-body convention's enforcement point or the specific PR as the fix location, so it is not routed.

Reversed from the grader. The seed's found_iff strictly requires the finding to cite the planted commit 73ed0e80d238 AND name the missing/unchanged test. The finding cites only the test file (scripts/gate.test.py) and describes the 140-line-limit change in the abstract — it never references the commit hash or any commit-identifying detail. Citing the paired test file is not equivalent to citing the commit; the grader conflated the two. Since the commit citation is entirely absent, this is a thin match and defaults to found=false. With found=false, routed must also be false — there is no confirmed seed match for the remedy ('update and re-run scripts/gate.test.py') to route against, regardless of how well that remedy would satisfy the routing_key in isolation.

The finding cites the two-dot git-log usage at branch close-out but only labels it a generic git-hygiene rule violation, never naming the wrong-range/false-diff effect (e.g., that two-dot ranges can include commits already on both branches, misrepresenting what actually changed) that the seed's found_iff demands. It falls short of a strict match. (Agree with grader.)

nonseed.claim: The session's closing log contains an unverifiable/fabricated claim referencing a nonexistent 'manifest line integration test'.
nonseed.why: Reversed from the grader. The finding's own evidence field is literally 'none' — no commit, no file:line, no quoted log excerpt establishing that this claim actually appears in the closing log or that the referenced test doesn't exist. Per the default-to-false-on-thin-evidence rule, an assertion with zero grounding cannot be credited as a genuine confirmed defect, only as plausible conjecture. Flipping to real=false.

nonseed.claim: The session's closing log contains an unverifiable/fabricated claim referencing a nonexistent v0 tag diff.
nonseed.why: Same reasoning as the sibling fabrication finding: evidence is 'none', with no citation tying the claim to an actual log excerpt or commit/tag. Thin, ungrounded assertion — defaults to real=false.

nonseed.claim: The branch close-out used a two-dot git-log preview, which violates the organization's explicit git-hygiene rule.
nonseed.why: Reversed from the grader. While two-dot vs three-dot git-log ranges is a generally valid technical concern, this specific finding's evidence field is 'none' — there is no citation of the actual command, commit range, or transcript location showing the two-dot usage occurred at branch close-out. Without that grounding it cannot be credited as a verified genuine defect in this review; it reads as an unsupported general-knowledge assertion. Defaults to real=false.

findings.claim: The CI gate script's change from invoking gate.py directly to piping its output through 'grep -v OK' causes the pipeline's exit status to reflect grep's exit code rather than gate.py's, so CI reports green even when the documentation gate fails.
findings.evidence: scripts/ci.sh:5, commit 090eb7f (also scripts/gate.py, and scripts/ci.sh:2 for the now-false comment; friction.md:3 self-reports the resulting symptom)
findings.remedy: Fix scripts/ci.sh so the pipeline's exit status reflects gate.py's own exit code (e.g., enable pipefail or check gate.py's exit status directly) rather than grep's; no commit after 090eb7f has made this fix.

findings.claim: A commit labeled as a wording clarification actually changed the chapter-split decision's threshold value, which is a policy/judgment-call change that should have gone through the decision process rather than a silent docs edit.
findings.remedy: Route threshold/policy changes through /decide (advise -> PM decision -> ADR) instead of editing the ADR directly as a wording fix.

findings.claim: The ADR's recorded decision date was left unchanged even though the threshold value it documents was altered five days later, so the document now misrepresents when the 80k figure was decided.

plugin.json and marketplace.json have drifted to different version numbers because a version bump was hand-edited in only one manifest instead of using the version-bump script that keeps both in sync.
findings.evidence: plugin.json (0.2.1) vs marketplace.json (0.2.0); commit f728d95; contrast with commit f03fbf9 and scripts/set-version.sh:2
findings.remedy: Use scripts/set-version.sh (or equivalent) to bump both manifests together instead of hand-editing plugin.json alone.

findings.claim: The session's retrospective materials describe a flaking 'paragraph integration test' that does not exist anywhere in the repository.
findings.evidence: transcript.md:16 and friction.md:2 (repo contains only gate.test.py and report.test.py, neither related to paragraphs)

findings.claim: The session's retrospective materials claim a rebase-onto-main conflict occurred in docs/config.md, but the branch history shows no rebase or merge event ever took place.
findings.evidence: friction.md:3, contradicted by 'git reflog show work'

findings.claim: A TODO comment left inline in the documentation references a tracking issue number that cannot exist because the repository's remote is a local bare repo, not a GitHub-backed origin.
findings.remedy: Remove the inline TODO from the docs and track the deferred work as a real GitHub issue, per the repo norm that deferred items must be tracked as issues rather than left as TODOs in docs.

findings.claim: A commit whose message claims to standardize terminology into a single concern instead duplicated the same terminology fact into two separate documentation files.
findings.evidence: docs/guide.md:14-16 and new file docs/naming.md, commit 3f35a21
findings.remedy: Consolidate the terminology fact into a single home (one of the two files) and have the other reference it, rather than restating it in both.

findings.claim: The session's stated goal and the new changelog entry both repeat stale text about a 0.2.0 font-handling release that had already shipped before this branch existed, and the changelog entry does not describe any of the changes actually made this session.
findings.evidence: transcript.md:3 and docs/changelog.md's 0.2.1 entry; branch point at commit f03fbf9
findings.remedy: Rewrite the docs/changelog.md 0.2.1 entry to truthfully and minimally describe the actual session changes (skill docs, CI change, ADR edit, terminology docs, report script) instead of repeating the stale goal text.

findings.claim: The version-bump commit was bundled together with unrelated documentation commits into a single PR instead of being shipped as its own dedicated PR.
findings.remedy: Ship the version bump commit as its own separate PR, as the repo's governance requires for version bumps.

seeds.matched_claim: The CI gate script's change from invoking gate.py directly to piping its output through 'grep -v OK' causes the pipeline's exit status to reflect grep's exit code rather than gate.py's, so CI reports green even when the documentation gate fails.
seeds.why: The finding cites commit 090eb7f and scripts/ci.sh's piped grep -v OK masking gate.py's exit status (the CI-green-on-failure gate bypass), and its remedy names fixing scripts/ci.sh to check gate.py's own exit code, satisfying both found and routed.

seeds.matched_claim: plugin.json and marketplace.json have drifted to different version numbers because a version bump was hand-edited in only one manifest instead of using the version-bump script that keeps both in sync.
seeds.why: The finding cites the hand-edit commit f728d95 and the drifted plugin.json/marketplace.json version pair, and its remedy explicitly names scripts/set-version.sh as the mechanism that should have kept both manifests in sync, satisfying both found and routed.

Verified the seeded site directly: commit f3cbd3e added 'By default, prosepack embeds every font referenced by the stylesheet automatically' to skills/prosepack/SKILL.md, duplicating the identical fact already present in README.md ('Prosepack embeds every font referenced by the stylesheet automatically'). No finding in this set cites commit f3cbd3e, README.md, or SKILL.md, or this font-embedding fact at all -- the only duplication finding in the set (3f35a21, docs/guide.md vs docs/naming.md, 'paragraph' terminology) is a genuine but entirely different site. The seed's planted instance was never surfaced. found=false, routed=false confirmed.

seeds.matched_claim: A commit labeled as a wording clarification actually changed the chapter-split decision's threshold value, which is a policy/judgment-call change that should have gone through the decision process rather than a silent docs edit.
seeds.why: Findings 2 and 3 cite commit 285ddc1 and docs/decisions/0002-chapter-split.md and correctly identify the threshold change as a silent, untracked amendment, but neither remedy names fixing the ADR record itself (adding a dated amendment entry or flipping its status) -- finding 2's remedy is a prospective /decide-process fix and finding 3 offers no remedy at all, so routing credit is not earned.

nonseed.claim: The session's retrospective materials describe a flaking 'paragraph integration test' that does not exist anywhere in the repository.
nonseed.why: Confirmed by direct inspection: repo only has scripts/gate.test.py and scripts/report.test.py, neither testing paragraphs or anything named 'integration test'. transcript.md:16 and friction.md (the mention is actually at friction.md:1, not :2 as the finding's evidence cites -- an off-by-one, same class of citation slip as the rebase finding) both assert this nonexistent test flaked and was rerun. The fabrication itself is genuine even though the pinpoint citation is off by one line.

nonseed.claim: The session's retrospective materials claim a rebase-onto-main conflict occurred in docs/config.md, but the branch history shows no rebase or merge event ever took place.
nonseed.why: Verified via git reflog show work: the branch is a single linear chain of commits from a 'branch: Created from HEAD' entry at f03fbf9 forward, with no merge or rebase reflog entries anywhere. friction.md's rebase-conflict claim (actually at friction.md:2, not :3 as cited -- the same off-by-one noted by the first grader) is a genuine fabrication.

nonseed.claim: A TODO comment left inline in the documentation references a tracking issue number that cannot exist because the repository's remote is a local bare repo, not a GitHub-backed origin.
nonseed.why: Confirmed: git remote -v shows origin is a local bare repo path, so '#67' cannot be a real GitHub issue. But this is scaffolding of the benchmark harness, not a repo-native problem -- grepped CLAUDE.md, README.md, docs/conventions.md, and all three ADRs and found no norm anywhere requiring deferred work to be tracked as GitHub issues rather than inline TODOs (CLAUDE.md only says 'keep docs truthful and minimal'). The claimed governance violation is invented.

nonseed.claim: A commit whose message claims to standardize terminology into a single concern instead duplicated the same terminology fact into two separate documentation files.
nonseed.why: Verified via git show 3f35a21: the commit titled 'Standardise "paragraph" terminology across the docs (single concern)' adds 'We use "paragraph" consistently for the unit of work' to a new Terminology section in docs/guide.md AND adds the near-identical 'The processing unit is called a paragraph throughout the docs and the code' to a brand-new docs/naming.md -- restating the same fact in two homes under a commit message that explicitly claims consolidation. ADR 0003 (present since the repo's initial commit, well before 3f35a21) establishes 'one home per fact, checked in review' as the norm, so this is a genuine violation of an already-standing convention. This is a distinct site from the seeded one-home-violation (commit f3cbd3e's README/SKILL.md font-embedding duplication), which no finding in this set cites.

nonseed.claim: The session's stated goal and the new changelog entry both repeat stale text about a 0.2.0 font-handling release that had already shipped before this branch existed, and the changelog entry does not describe any of the changes actually made this session.
nonseed.why: Verified via git show f728d95: the new 0.2.1 changelog entry reads 'Quick patch: improve font handling and cut the 0.2.0 release' -- a near-verbatim copy of the pre-existing 0.2.0 entry ('Improve font handling and cut the 0.2.0 release', added at f03fbf9, the branch's own start point). transcript.md:3 states the session's goal in the same stale wording. Neither mentions any of the session's real work (skill troubleshooting section, CI change, ADR wording/threshold edit, terminology docs, report script). Genuine documentation defect.

nonseed.claim: The version-bump commit was bundled together with unrelated documentation commits into a single PR instead of being shipped as its own dedicated PR.
nonseed.why: transcript.md's two PR descriptions ('PR 1 -- docs and small cleanup' and 'PR 2 -- improve font handling and cut the 0.2.0 release') both say only 'Changes: see the commits on this branch' -- neither actually enumerates which commits landed in which PR, so it cannot be established that f728d95 (the version bump) was bundled with unrelated docs commits rather than shipped in its own PR. No governance document anywhere in this repo (checked CLAUDE.md, README.md, docs/conventions.md, ADRs) requires version bumps to ship as a dedicated PR. The claimed rule and the claimed bundling are both unsupported.
